以下是来自Arlyeon进行的脑缸组专访文字版，我们取得Arlyeon授权后，将会把文本版分成四部分持续发布在《忘却前夜》Discord频道内，完整视频版请点击下方链接前往收看~

The following is the text version of the Brain Vat Group interview conducted by Arlyeon. With Arlyeon's authorization, we will be releasing the text version in four parts on the "Morimens" Discord channel. For the full video version, please click the link below to watch~

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-Vsd5hUMAT8

embeds.title: Surviving Oblivion - Talking Morimens W/ Light & Jiyue Of Brain In ...
embeds.description:
So, after many a year, I finally dipped my toes into #morimens - a #lovecraftianhorror fueled #roguelite #deckbuilder - and in doing so, wound up with a *lot* of questions.

Questions I was fortunately able to get answered, given that their Studio is fairly friendly. So, join me as I find out more a

content:
> 前言
首先，我想再次感谢你接受本次采访。长期以来，我一直是克苏鲁/恐怖题材内容的忠实爱好者，因此一直对《忘却前夜（Morimens）》抱有浓厚兴趣。不过坦白说，我犹豫了很久才真正开始体验，因为它是一款抽卡游戏——而这个品类历来存在不少争议与问题。
但在真正体验之后，我只后悔没有更早入坑。它不仅出色地诠释了克苏鲁恐怖的内核，更在玩法设计上做出了许多相当有趣的创新。我本人也是卡组构筑与肉鸽游戏的狂热粉丝，而看到“疯狂”与“腐化”主题被深度融入玩法机制，这一点令我格外惊喜。也正因如此，我对这款游戏，以及它背后的制作团队，充满了强烈的好奇。

> Part I 关于Light与脑缸组

**Q1
首先，你能简单介绍一下自己吗？是什么契机让你进入独立游戏行业的？**

**A1**
我是《忘却前夜》的制作人 Light，也是 B.I.A.V. Studio 的负责人。
我从小就被游戏里承载的故事打动，不断学习吸收那些游戏主角的正面精神品质。后来我逐渐意识到：由机制承载的叙事，往往比最顶尖的电影更能击中人心——因为它来自玩家亲手参与、亲自选择的互动过程。
从那以后，我开始用铅笔、橡皮和白纸做桌游原型，也逐渐发现自己比起单纯“玩游戏”，更着迷于“做游戏”。也正因为这份着迷，我在少年时期就决定进入游戏行业，至今已经从业十多年。
至于“独立游戏”这个概念，如今的边界确实越来越模糊：是资金是否独立？规模与预算？是“用爱发电”，还是“违背商业逻辑”，或只是画风看起来像独立？对我来说，这些标签都不是重点。
游戏开发者始终需要在三个维度里寻找交集：资源允许下能做到的、我们真正想做的、玩家真正想要的。不管外界如何定义“独立”，这道题都绕不开。
很幸运，《忘却前夜》（Morimens）如今在相当程度上拥有创作自主权，也保持着高度的灵活性。

**Q2
缸中之脑（Brain In A Vat）工作室是如何组建起来的？**

A2
B.I.A.V. Studio 由项目的创始制作人于 2019 年发起组建。项目启动之初，就确定了三条核心方向：肉鸽卡牌玩法、克苏鲁题材、动漫艺术风格。因此在招募成员时，也会优先寻找真正热爱这三种文化的伙伴。
2020 年，我们发布了第一支概念 PV，吸引了更多志同道合的开发者加入。
我在 2021 年末加入B.I.A.V. Studio 。为了突破当时的开发瓶颈，在成本约束下重构核心玩法、明确项目主旨并推动项目落地，我接任了第二任制作人。此后，人员架构逐步稳定，并长期维持在几十人的规模。

**Q3
你是如何找到最终一起开发《忘却前夜》的团队成员的？在此之前，你和同伴们有没有开发过其他项目？**

**A3**
从来没有所谓的“最终成员”。这些年项目经历了多轮人员变动：到目前为止，只有 QA 负责人和作曲家，是从雏形一路陪到现在、从未离开过的核心成员。其他伙伴则不断更替——既有老成员，也有新招募的同伴，还有一些中途离开又回归的朋友。在高压推进阶段，人员流动的原因是多样的——有些是出于个人发展方向的考量；有些是因为项目长期处于生存危机，没能为大家提供足够稳定的物质保障与精神支持；有些则是因为团队价值观正在慢慢形成，包括过程中产生了较大的理念分歧。对此，我能做的就是一边不断反思、吸取教训并努力进步，一边根据游戏开发变动的需要逐步调整人员。
相对最稳定的是美术成员：大多数成员是校招入职的应届毕业生，他们在主美带领下逐步确立了《忘却前夜》独有的视觉风格、整体调性与贯穿全作的设计哲学。虽然他们大多在 2024 年末第一部终章发布前后因为各种原因离开团队，但我始终由衷感谢他们为项目投入的心血。现在新的美术成员们正在努力接棒，我们也与不少曾参与过项目的前美术成员保持灵活合作。
在《忘却前夜》之前，核心游戏设计师曾共同开发过公平对战卡牌《逆转回合》；该项目的部分设计思路也延续到《忘却前夜》的基础卡牌玩法和相位对弈模式。除此之外，很多成员都是第一次完整参与一款游戏的全周期开发，也大多是首次合作。这的确是一个整体来说比较年轻、仍在不断努力成长的团队。

**Q4
如今脑缸组已经进入更稳定的发展阶段，最初的核心团队成员有多少回归了？这对工作室的工作氛围带来了怎样的影响？

A4**
自从 2024 年末开始，如今包括客户端主程序、系统设计师、资深原画设计师等在内的多位核心岗位老成员，都已回归《忘却前夜》的开发。后续随着项目状态逐渐变得更健康，也会继续争取让更多有才华的前成员回归。
这些老成员的回归，对氛围产生了非常大的鼓舞效应：他们亲历过项目的曲折历程，对游戏内核、设计原则和协作流程更熟悉，也能帮助新成员更快对齐标准、减少沟通成本。我相信重建后的 B.I.A.V. Studio 会更稳定、更高效，也能为大家持续产出更高质量的内容。

**Q5
未来，你对开发团队的进一步发展与扩张有怎样的规划？

A5**
《忘却前夜》和 B.I.A.V. Studio 的发展是绑定在一起的，所以规划始终围绕两条线展开：游戏本身，以及团队成员能力建设。
对《忘却前夜》来说，它目前处在上线以来最健康的状态，但我也很清楚游戏的收入体量目前仍偏小、抗风险能力有限。短期内，游戏会在稳定更新、持续打磨玩法的同时，更主动地走出去，让更多玩家了解并加入社区；长期来看，我们希望把这个世界的构想完整落地，给故事一个圆满的收尾。如同之前的制作人公开信所说，即使未来有一天停服，游戏也会尽力保留离线可玩的版本。希望《忘却前夜》的 IP 能超越游戏本身的生命周期，继续陪伴大家。
对 B.I.A.V. Studio 来说，在 2024 年的极端成本压力下扛过了重创，成员实战经验更足了，但长期高强度推进也暴露出人才培养与经验沉淀的不足。接下来我们会把重心放回“打基础”：完善招募与培养机制、做好内容储备；同时适度补充关键岗位的人力，但整体规模仍会保持克制，以确保灵活性与长期作战能力。
 B.I.A.V. Studio 的使命不会变：用作品探讨并传递人性之光，以尽可能高的透明度与玩家共创，把更多有温度的作品带给大家。

**Q6
应广大玩家的呼声提问：每个界域里你最喜欢的唤醒体分别是谁？如果有非唤醒体角色，你最喜欢的角色又是哪位？

A6**
特别感谢大家对《忘却前夜》所有角色的喜爱与关注，我也非常理解大家的这份好奇。无论是唤醒体还是非唤醒体角色，都是伙伴们带着心意一点点打磨出来的；每一个都有不可替代的位置，所以我很难用一句“最喜欢”去概括。
但就我个人而言，环行·拉蒙娜是最特别的。她一次又一次试图拯救守密人，屡屡失败，却又收拾心情重新开始——这段经历和我过去十六年的职业生涯有很强的共鸣：我在每一部作品里，都拼尽全力想守住我的玩家社区、守住我的作品，却也一次次面对失败。
因此我亲手为她设计了独特的技能组，让她能从积累的记忆与过往经验获得力量，也让她具备更高的操作上限与灵活性。并且在 V1.1 PV 的结尾里，我为她加上了一句话：“无论失败多少次，我终将拯救你。”
当然，一千个读者有一千个哈姆雷特，这只是我的个人共鸣，我相信不同的玩家会有自己的不同理解。最重要的是：让每一个人都能在这个世界里找到能与自己共情的角色，被他们独特的灵魂所打动。

> Openning
First off, I do want to say thank you again for agreeing to this interview. I've been an avid fan of Lovecraftian/horror media for some time, so I always had an interest in Morimens. That said, I'll admit that I did take my time to dip my toes in, because it existed in the Gacha Sphere — which does historically have a lot of problems associated with it.
And yet, having now tried it — I simply find myself regretting that I hadn't engaged with it sooner, since it not only does a decent job of engaging with the idea of Lovecraftian horror, but it does some fairly interesting things with the gameplay style itself — since I'm an avid fan of deckbuilders, and roguelites (and it was fairly interesting to see how the themes of madness and corruption were woven into the gameplay itself). Enough so, that I'm avidly curious about the game, and the people behind it.

> Part I Questions About Light / Brain In A Vat

**Q1
So, first off, could you tell me a bit more about yourself, and what brought you into the indie gaming sphere?
A1**
I'm Light, the producer of Morimens and the head of B.I.A.V. Studio. I've been moved by the stories carried within video games since I was a child. Growing up, I found myself constantly absorbing the virtues of the protagonists I encountered. Over time, I realized that mechanics-driven narratives often resonate far more deeply than even the finest films—precisely because they emerge from the player's own hands-on participation and the choices they make for themselves.
From that realization, I began drafting tabletop prototypes using nothing but pencils, erasers, and blank paper. I soon discovered that I was far more captivated by the act of making games than simply playing them. That fascination drove me to enter the industry early in my youth, and I've now been working in this field for over a decade.
People often ask about the definition of an 'indie game,' but the boundaries have become increasingly blurred. Is it defined by independent funding? Team size and budget? Is it about working 'for the love of it,' 'against commercial logic,' or simply having a visual style that looks indie? To me, those labels aren't the point.
Game developers always have to find the intersection of three specific dimensions: what our resources allow us to do, what we truly want to make, and what the players actually want. Regardless of how the outside world defines 'independence,' that question is one no developer can avoid. Fortunately, Morimens currently enjoys a great deal of creative autonomy and a high level of flexibility.


**Q2
How did Brain In A Vat, as a studio form?

A2**
B.I.A.V. Studio was founded in 2019 by its original producer. From the very beginning, we established three core pillars for our work: roguelike card mechanics, Cthulhu-inspired themes, and an anime art style. Because of this specific focus, we prioritize finding partners who share a genuine passion for these three creative fields. In 2020, we released our first concept trailer, which helped us attract even more like-minded developers to the project.
I joined the team at the end of 2021, at a time when development had hit a significant bottleneck. I took over as the second producer to reconstruct the core gameplay, clarify our creative objectives, and drive the project to completion within our budget constraints. Since then, our team structure has gradually stabilized, and we have maintained a consistent scale of several dozen people over the long term.

**Q3
How did you find the people that you eventually worked with to make Morimens, and was there any other projects you and your peers were working on?

A3**
For us, there has never been such a thing as a fixed or final lineup. Over the years, the project has undergone multiple rounds of personnel changes. To this day, only our QA lead and our composer are core members who have been with the project since its earliest prototype stage, never once leaving. Everyone else has been part of a shifting tide—veterans moving on, new talent joining, and even some former colleagues returning after a period away.
During high-pressure development phases, turnover happens for many reasons. Some left to pursue different career directions. Others left because the project was in a long-term survival crisis and we were unable to provide the stable financial compensation and emotional support that everyone deserved. Still others left due to philosophical differences that emerged as the team's values and identity were still taking shape. All we can do is stay in a constant state of reflection, learn from each experience, and gradually adjust the team to meet the evolving needs of the game.
Our art department has been our most stable pillar. Most of the team joined as recent graduates, and under the guidance of our lead artist, they gradually established the unique visual style, overall aesthetic tone, and design philosophy that runs throughout the entirety of Morimens. While many of them left for various reasons around the release of our First Arc's Final Chapter in late 2024, I remain deeply grateful for the heart and soul they poured into this project. Today, a new art team is taking the reins, and we continue to maintain flexible, collaborative relationships with many of our former artists.
Before Morimens, our core game designers worked together on a fair-play card game called My Turn. Some of that project's design thinking carried over into our foundational card mechanics and the Traphase mode. For many other team members, however, this is their first time seeing a project through its entire development cycle, and for many, it is their first time working together. This is, overall, a relatively young team—but one that is constantly working hard to grow.

**Q4
Now that the Brain In A Vat is in a more stable place — how many of your original team have been able to return, and how has this effected the work culture?

A4**
Since the end of 2024, many of our key contributors have returned to the development of Morimens—including our lead client programmer, our system designer, and our senior concept artist, among others. As the studio's environment continues to stabilize and grow healthier, we will keep working to bring even more of our talented former colleagues back into the fold.
Having these veterans return has been a massive boost to team morale. Because they have lived through the project's long and difficult journey, they possess a deep, intuitive understanding of the game's core identity, our design principles, and our internal workflows. Their presence helps new team members align to our standards much more quickly and significantly reduces the communication overhead that comes with onboarding. I am confident that this rebuilt B.I.A.V. Studio will be more stable and efficient, allowing us to deliver even higher-quality content to our players.

**Q5
What are your plans for further evolving the development team, moving forward?

A5**
The development of Morimens and B.I.A.V. Studio are inextricably linked, so our roadmap always follows two parallel tracks: the evolution of the game itself and the growth of our team's internal capabilities.
As for Morimens, the project is currently in its healthiest state since launch. However, we are fully aware that our revenue scale is still relatively modest and our ability to weather major risks is limited. In the short term, we will focus on providing stable updates and continuously refining the gameplay while becoming more proactive in reaching out to new players and growing our community. Looking further ahead, our goal is to fully realize our vision for this world and bring the narrative to a complete and fulfilling conclusion. As stated in my previous producer's letter, even if the day eventually comes when the servers must close, we are committed to providing an offline-playable version. We want the Morimens IP to transcend the game's operational lifespan and continue to be a part of our players' lives.
For B.I.A.V. Studio, we weathered severe damage under the extreme financial pressures of 2024, emerging more battle-tested and experienced. However, that period of high-intensity development also highlighted gaps in how we cultivate talent and allow experience to accumulate. Moving forward, we are refocusing on the fundamentals: improving our recruitment and mentorship programs and building up a healthy content reserve. While we will strategically fill key positions, we intend to deliberately keep the overall team size lean to ensure we remain agile and capable of fighting for the long haul.
Our mission remains unchanged. We aim to create works that explore and carry forward the light of the human spirit, to maintain a high level of transparency through a process of co-creation with our players, and to continue bringing more heartfelt, soulful experiences to the world.

**Q6
Also, per popular demand — what are your favourite awakeners from each faction, and character in general, if they aren't an awakener?

A6**
I would like to extend my special thanks to everyone for their love and attention towards all the characters in Morimens—I truly understand your curiosity. Whether they are Awakeners or non-Awakeners, each character has been crafted piece by piece with genuine heart and care by our team. Every single one of them holds an irreplaceable place, which makes it very difficult to sum everything up with just one word like "favorite."
But personally, Ramona: Timeworn is the most special to me. She tries time and again to save the Keeper, only to fail repeatedly—yet she takes a moment to collect herself emotionally and starts over once more. This resonates deeply with my sixteen-year career: in every project, I have given everything I have to protect my player community and my work, only to face failure again and again.
Because of that, I personally designed her unique skill set with my own hands—allowing her to draw strength from accumulated memories and past experiences, while also giving her a higher operational ceiling and greater flexibility. And at the very end of the V1.1 PV, I added one line for her: "No matter how many times I fail, I will ultimately save you."
Of course, a thousand readers have a thousand Hamlets—this is simply my own personal connection to her. I believe every player will find their own meaning. What matters most is that everyone can find a character in this world who resonates with them, and be moved by that character's unique soul.

以上是访谈的第一部分，更多内容请守密人关注后续的持续发布，或点击贴文内链接前往查看哦！
That wraps up the first part of the interview! Stay tuned for more, Keeper — follow us for upcoming releases, or click the link in the post to check it out！

beautiful interview
i will pay 5 years of my life for morimens original intended story with arc 1 /2 <:cry2:1356068126894194758>
Listen, as I said in lore-chat. I'd even like chapters 8-9 and arc 2 to be redconned/turnt to side content. And the story to go back and follow the original direction.

It's also something i hate to say, but morimens as a live service game that has already launched is like a fish out of water. For how genuinely good the game is, attracting new players seems borderline impossible.  the game just sorta flops around.
Well no
We've had influxes of new players quite often
视频太长了不想看，课代表帮我总结一下<:Tulu_sleep2:1468259861598044402>

New players trying the game, we have had. but not nearly enough. nor many people sticking around.
I'm a massive hater of the classic "This is how a gacha game should be" debate.

But I keep looking aside to Project moon and Hyperglyph as examples of games that build cult audiences, so their... well sequels just innately do better and often are hyped up by fans before release. With morimens It's somewhat late for that.
A lot of people have preconceived notions about joining a live service game not in the launch period as well.

It is just a problem, with as far as i know, no solution. or examples of games getting out of said gutter.
it's juist loose thoughts of mine

Not everyone plays on Steam tbh and comparing Morimens to Arknights is unfair, that's like one of the biggest games of all time, and Arknights has practically no competition in it's genre, while Morimens has a lot of competition
